diagnosticQuestions.prompt: If you only had ONE price from yesterday, what would be missing compared to a full candle?
choices: Nothing — one price tells the whole day
You’d miss the high, low, open, and how the day moved between them
You’d only miss the company name
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Think: start line, finish line, best moment, worst moment.

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: The thick middle of a candle (the body) is mainly about:
choices: Where the stock opened vs closed in that period
How many people own the stock
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Body connects two important prices at the start and end of the slice of time.

teachSlides.title: Picture a single race
teachSlides.body: One candle is a snapshot for a time you pick (1 minute, 1 day, whatever). It answers: Where did we start? Where did we end? How high did we go? How low did we go?

teachSlides.title: The four corners
teachSlides.body: Open: where the slice began. Close: where it ended. High: the top wick tip (best price reached). Low: the bottom wick tip (worst price reached). Together they are often called OHLC.

teachSlides.title: Why it’s not magic
teachSlides.body: A candle describes what already happened. It doesn’t predict tomorrow by itself — it’s a picture you use with context (trend, levels, and your rules).

recapSlide.title: You’re ahead — quick recap
recapSlide.body: One candle = open, high, low, close for that period. The body links open and close; wicks show the extremes. It’s history on a stick, not a crystal ball.

masteryQuestions.explanation: Those four prices define the candle for that period.

masteryQuestions.prompt: The thin lines above/below the body are often called:
masteryQuestions.explanation: Wicks show how far price stretched beyond the open–close range.

masteryQuestions.prompt: A candle by itself is best thought of as:
choices: A record of trading over a chosen time window
A message from the exchange about your account
masteryQuestions.explanation: It encodes past prices; interpretation needs context and risk rules.
